primary_concept: Scoping an app into phases using MVP thinking — building only what is needed first, then adding features intentionally in a second phase
secondary_concepts: Translating a vague idea into a structured plan (goal, input, output, layout, features)
Distinguishing functional vs. cosmetic issues when debugging
Saving work at stable checkpoints before making new changes
Recognizing and avoiding context rot by starting fresh chats between phases
mvp_features: Display a short passage of text for the user to type
Capture keystrokes and compare them against the target text in real time
Highlight correct characters in green and incorrect characters in red as the user types
Show a completion screen with words-per-minute (WPM) and accuracy percentage when the passage is finished
Provide a restart button to reset the session with the same passage
stretch_features: Multiple built-in passages the user can choose from
A difficulty selector that changes passage length or complexity
A running WPM counter displayed live during typing
Visual or audio feedback on completion (e.g., a congratulations animation)
Keyboard heatmap showing which keys the user mistyped most often
forbidden_features: Saving high scores or session history — requires localStorage or a database, which is not introduced until Module 2
A user account or leaderboard — requires a database and identity management, covered in Module 2 and beyond
Fetching passages from an external API — requires API integration, which is not introduced until Module 3
LLM-generated passages or feedback — requires an LLM API, which is not introduced until Module 3
Any form of data persistence between sessions — this is the defining constraint of Module 1
learner_outcome: Learners will be able to plan, scope, and build the Touch Typing app across two deliberate phases — writing a structured PRD before coding, making intentional decisions about what to include in the MVP versus what to defer, and recovering from a broken state using versioning and a fresh chat context.
instructor_talking_points: Start by writing a one-paragraph plain-English description of the app before opening the coding tool — this demonstrates why structured planning produces better first results than jumping straight into prompting

When scoping Phase 1, explicitly cut the high-score feature and explain why: saving data requires persistence, which the learner does not know yet — this is the clearest live example of a forbidden feature in Module 1
After Phase 1 is working, demonstrate saving the project before making any Phase 2 changes — reinforce the habit of checkpointing at stable moments

If a cosmetic bug appears (e.g., text color not quite right), name it as cosmetic and deprioritize it in favor of a functional issue — this models the triage skill introduced in Video 1 and formalized in Video 5
scope_warnings: Adding a high-score tracker: tempting because typing games always have leaderboards, but storing scores between sessions requires localStorage or a database — this belongs in Module 2
Fetching random passages from a quote or text API: sounds like a small quality-of-life improvement, but it requires an external API call — this belongs in Module 3
Adding a login so users can compare scores: requires both a database and authentication — two concepts well beyond Module 1 scope

Building a full settings panel (font size, theme, sound toggles): these are cosmetic features that multiply prompt complexity without teaching any new planning concept — typical scope creep in the UI polish phase

Asking the AI to generate personalized passages using an LLM: conflates the coding tool (used for building) with an LLM as a product feature — this distinction is explicitly taught in Module 3 Video 4 and should not appear in Module 1
